title: Detecting Odd Length Cycles in a Directed Graph

statement.markdown: Given a directed graph represented as an adjacency list, detect whether there exists an odd length cycle in the graph. The graph contains vertices numbered from 0 to n-1, where n is the number of vertices in the graph. Each edge in the graph is represented as a pair of vertices, where the first vertex is the source and the second vertex is the destination.
statement.inputFormat: The input will consist of n, the number of vertices, followed by n-1 edges, where each edge is represented as a pair of vertices.
statement.outputFormat: Output 1 if the graph contains an odd length cycle, otherwise output 0.
statement.notes: You can use DFS or BFS to solve this problem.

examples.explanation: The graph contains an odd length cycle: 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0.

hints.text: Use DFS to traverse the graph and detect the presence of an odd length cycle.

editorial.title: Detecting Odd Length Cycles in a Directed Graph
editorial.intuition: The key insight here is to use a visited array to keep track of the vertices we have visited so far. If we revisit a vertex and its previous visit was via an edge, it means we have found a cycle.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: Enumerate all possible paths and check if any of them form a cycle.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use DFS to traverse the graph and detect the presence of an odd length cycle. We can use a visited array to keep track of the vertices we have visited so far and a recursive function to perform the DFS traversal.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of this approach follows from the fact that a cycle in a graph exists if and only if there is a path from a vertex back to itself.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: 1. Read the input: Read the number of vertices and the edges from the input. 2. Initialize the visited array: Initialize an array to keep track of the vertices we have visited so far. 3. Perform DFS: Use a recursive function to perform the DFS traversal of the graph.
commonMistakes: Forget to reset the visited array between DFS traversals.
Use a visited array of the wrong size.
